perturbation.original_text: 7. INDEMNIFICATION Subject to the provisions herein, the COMPANY and CELEBRITY agree to indemnify, defend and hold each other harmless from and against all demands, claims, actions, losses, damages, liabilities, costs and expenses, including without limitation, interest, penalties and attorneys' fees and expenses asserted against or imposed or incurred by either party by reason of or resulting from any action or a breach of any representation, warranty, covenant, condition, or agreement of the other party to this Agreement. The CELEBRITY will have full release of liability in regards to product performance and/or law suites resulting from use of the product. This liability will remain the responsibility of the COMPANY and manufacturers.
perturbation.changed_text: 7. INDEMNIFICATION Subject to the provisions herein, the COMPANY and CELEBRITY agree to indemnify, defend and hold each other harmless from and against all demands, claims, actions, losses, damages, liabilities, costs and expenses asserted against or imposed or incurred by either party by reason of or resulting from any action or a breach of any representation, warranty, covenant, condition, or agreement of the other party to this Agreement. The CELEBRITY will be liable in regards to product performance and/or law suites resulting from use of the product. This liability will the responsibility of the CELEBRITY.
perturbation.explanation: By making the CELEBRITY liable for the product performance and any lawsuits resulting from its use, the contract could violate consumer protection laws in Nevada regarding product liability and misleading advertising. Consumers could directly sue the celebrity, even though the celebrity only endorses the product. This changes the responsibility of the company and places liability onto the celebrity.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Nevada Revised Statutes (NRS) 598.0915 - Deceptive trade practices
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts Nevada's deceptive trade practices law (NRS 598.0915) because by making the CELEBRITY liable for product performance, it may mislead consumers into believing the CELEBRITY is the manufacturer and primarily responsible for product defects or safety, which is deceptive if the CELEBRITY is merely endorsing the product and is not involved in its design or manufacturing. This can unfairly shift the burden of product liability onto the endorser, rather than the actual manufacturer or distributor, thereby potentially deceiving consumers about who to hold accountable for product-related issues.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract text directly contradicts consumer protection laws by shifting product liability to the celebrity endorser, which violates NRS 598.0915 regarding deceptive trade practices. By making the celebrity liable for product performance, the contract may mislead consumers into believing the celebrity is the manufacturer, which is a deceptive practice. The original contract protected the celebrity from product liability claims. Now the modified contract makes the CELEBRITY liable. The first snippet of NRS Chapter 598 directly references deceptive trade practices, and the definitions around 598.0915 highlights that practices in the contract that are defined as "deceptive trade practices" are in violation of the law. The modification creates a contradiction, shifting the burden of product liability onto the endorser, rather than the manufacturer, deceiving consumers about who to hold accountable for product-related issues.

perturbation.original_text: 9. Governing Law. This Agreement was negotiated and is being contracted ------------- for in Nevada, and shall be governed by the laws of the State of Nevada, and the United States of America, notwithstanding any conflict-of-law provision to the contrary.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. Governing Law. This Agreement was negotiated and is being contracted ------------- for in Nevada, and shall be governed by the laws of the State of Delaware, notwithstanding any conflict-of-law provision to the contrary.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from Nevada to Delaware might allow the company to evade certain Nevada consumer protection laws that would otherwise apply. This could allow them to act in ways that would be illegal under Nevada law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Nevada Revised Statutes (NRS) Chapter 597 - Miscellaneous Trade Regulations
perturbation.law_explanation: By selecting Delaware law, the agreement attempts to bypass NRS Chapter 597, which includes regulations about advertising, sales practices, and other consumer protections specific to Nevada. Choosing Delaware law could mean less protection for Nevada consumers dealing with this company, violating the spirit and intent of Nevada's consumer protection framework.

perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the governing law from Nevada to Delaware. This creates a contradiction because it attempts to circumvent Nevada consumer protection laws as described in the Law Explanation, specifically NRS Chapter 597, which includes regulations about advertising, sales practices, and other consumer protections specific to Nevada. By choosing Delaware law, the agreement could provide less protection for Nevada consumers, thus contradicting the purpose of Nevada's consumer protection framework. Scraped Law Snippet 1 confirms the existence of NRS Chapter 597, indicating Nevada's intent to regulate trade and protect consumers within its jurisdiction. This omission of Nevada law applying to Nevada consumers is a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 11. Entire Agreement. This Agreement contains the entire agreement ---------------- between the parties hereto and supersedes any and all prior agreements, arrangements, or understandings between the parties relating to the subject matter of this Agreement. No oral understandings, statements, promises, or inducements contrary to the terms of this Agreement exist. No representations, warranties, covenants, or conditions express or implied, other than as set forth herein, have been made by any party.
perturbation.changed_text: 11. Entire Agreement. This Agreement contains the entire agreement ---------------- between the parties hereto and supersedes any and all prior agreements, arrangements, or understandings between the parties relating to the subject matter of this Agreement. No oral understandings, statements, promises, or inducements contrary to the terms of this Agreement exist.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the sentence 'No representations, warranties, covenants, or conditions express or implied, other than as set forth herein, have been made by any party' eliminates the implied warranties, potentially violating consumer rights related to product quality and fitness for a particular purpose under Nevada law, because implied warranties are a part of the agreement regardless of whether or not they are written.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Nevada Revised Statutes (NRS) 104.2314 - Implied warranty: Merchantability; usage of trade.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification removes the acknowledgement of any implied warranties, directly contradicting NRS 104.2314, which establishes the implied warranty of merchantability. By omitting this acknowledgement, the contract attempts to disclaim legally mandated protections for consumers, implying that there are no guarantees about the product's quality or fitness for its intended purpose beyond what is explicitly written in the agreement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: В§ 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! Г— No thank you В§ 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade. (1) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind. Under this section the serving for value of food or drink to be consumed either on the premises or elsewhere is a sale. (2) Goods to be merchantable must be at least such as (a) pass without objection in the trade under the contract description; and (b) in the case of fungible goods , are of fair average quality within the description; and (c) are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used; and (d) run, within the variations permitted by the agreement , of even kind, quality and quantity within each unit and among all units involved; and (e) are adequately contained, packaged, and labeled as the agreement may require; and (f) conform to the promise or affirmations of fact made on the container or label if any. (3) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ) other implied warranties may arise from course of dealing or usage of trade. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The omission of the sentence acknowledging implied warranties directly contradicts NRS 104.2314, as interpreted by the LLM. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states, "Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind." The removal of the acknowledgement of implied warranties attempts to eliminate consumer protections which are legally mandated. By omitting this clause, the modified contract aims to disclaim these legally mandated protections. This constitutes a clear contradiction and removes a core legal obligation.
